BY THE cm·~lISSION: 
He have been asked the following 
questions by Charles Scully, General Counsel of the California

1. 
If both officers and member s of t.he staff of 
the Federation are lobbyists, may the Federa-
t.ion, through its standing corn .. raittee on political 
education, engage in its traditional political 
act.ivi ties?

on political education, engage in its traditional

tion are lobbyists, may the Federation, through

engage in its traditional political activities? 
4~ 
If the answer to each of the first three questions 
is negative, then is there any manner in which 
the Federation may continue to engage in its 
traditional political activities and also continue 
to attempt to influence legislative and cdl:nnlstra-
tive cction?

Federation is contained in the analysis. 
cm:JCLUSIO?:J 
Nothing in the Poli tical Reform l\ct pn:::vents the Federa-
tion from continuing to participate fully in the political 
process.

members of the Executive Councilor officers of the Federation 
act as lobbyists. 
However, those individuals who are lobbyists 
may not participate in any activities of the Federation which"

and may not serve on COPE, the Federation's political arm. 
ANALYSIS 
I. 
The questions we have been asked are important not only 
because of the large size of the Federation and the important 
role it has historically performed in political carnpaigns , ij 
but also because the questions call for elaboration of the fun-
damental nature of the regulation' of lobbyists embodied in the 
Political Reform Act. 
In essence, we are asked whether it is 
possible uI,der the Act for a mass membership organization to 
represent the interests of its meIT~ers before the Legislature 
and administrative agencies and at the sa.rne time make contribu-
tions to state officials and candidates and otherv:ise rer:1ain 
~activ~ in the political process~ 
As we shall_see; the regula-
/' tion of lobbyists ir:1posed by the _L\ct I 
,-.-hich protects important 
interests of the public in assuring integrity and impartiality 
in government, is fully consistent with the continu~tion by the 
Federation of its traditional lobbying and political activities . 
The Political Reform Act attempts to diminish the improper 
personal influence of lobbyists by prohibiting them from making 
contributions to state officials and candidates. 
. 
1/ 
It shall be unla'.-;ful for a lobbyist to make a 
contribution [to a state candidate, a 
co~mittee 
supporting a state candidate, or an elected state 
Officer) I or to act as an agent or intermediary 
in the making of any [such] contribution or to 
arrange for the making of any [such] contribution 
by himself or by any other person. 
Section 86202 
-All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless othen'lise noted. 
2/ 
-The Federation is the largest labor organization in the 
state," representing through its affiliates over one million 
members. 
In 1974 it reported spending more than $150 / 000 in 
behalf 6f state candidates and measures alone. 


'No. 75-004 
~a,ge Three 
This and other provisions of the Political Refonu Act 
are premised on the idea that a lobbyist is an advocate before a 
tribunal. 
An advocate should not be bearing gifts. 
He should 
succeed or fail on the merits of his position and the persua-
si veness of his argUt:1ents I not on 
ho'.v~ much [,tonev he ca; con tr i-
bute to a political campaign. 
The Political Re~orm Act severed 
the link between lobbying and making contributions. 
Individuals 
"\ ho vish to lobby must conceT'.trate their efforts on 2dvocacy. 
Individuals who wish to make contributions may not b~ lobbyists. 
On·the other hand, the Act does not prohibit org2nizations 
"lhich employ lobbyists from making contributions. 
Orgcmizations r 
including those which employ lobbyists I are free to IT.ake contribu-
tions so long as they are disclosed and so long as the lobbyists 
do not themselves participate in the making or arr2nging of con-
tributions. 
Wi th these general principles in mi.nd t He turn next to 
a description of the structure of the Federation. 
II. 
p~' 
The California Labor Federation is a state central body 
/' which is chartered by the national A:Ll[;rican Federation of Labor 
and Congress of Irldust:cial Orgarl.izations (A:FL--CIO) . 
. 1 
The Federai.:ion consif.;ts of organi~ations loca't.ed in 
California which are: 
a) local unions chartered direc~ly by 
the 1I.FL-CIO or by the national or intern 
onal lL'1ions affili-· 
ated VIi th the l\FL-CIO I 
b) central labor bodies chartered :by the 
AFL-CIO, c) councils and joint boards chartered by national or 
international unions affiliated with the AFL-CIO, and d) such 
other subordinate bodies that the Executive Council may deter-
mine are eligible for affiliation (California Labor Federation, 
AFL-CIO, C037titution, H.ules and Order of Business, 1\rtic1e III, 
Section 1).-
The supreme governing body of the Federation is the 
convention, which meets regularly every' two years and specially 
to consider endorsement of candidates and sta 
de propositions 
(Cons ti tution, l',rti cle XV 1 Sections 1, 2). 
Het'.-leen meetings 
of the convention, the affairs of the Federa 
on are directed 
by the Executive Coun 
1 (Constitution, Article IX, Section 2) , 
\-Ihich consists of the officers of the Federation -

t, 
a Secretary-Treasurer, a General Vice President and 34 additional 
ferences herein to "Constitution" are to the constitu-
tion of the California Labor Federation, unless the contrary is

Vice Presidents (Constitution, Article IX, Section 1; Article IV, 
Section 1). 
The first three named officers are full-time and 
salaried. 
The remaining members of the Executive Council are 
not salaried, but are entitled under the Constitution to rei~­
bursement for expenses when attending Executive Council meetings 
and $35 per day compensation plus expenses when officially 
authorized to work on business of the Federation 
(Co~stitution, 
Article ,XI, Sections lf 2). 
The Secretary-Treasurer is the 
chief executive officer of the Federation (Constitution, Article VII, 
Section 1). 
The Executive Courlcil instructs the Secretary-Treasurer 
to introduce legislation and assists the Secretary-Treasurer in 
carrying out his legislative responsibilities (Constitution, 
'Article IX, Section 4). 
One of the Executive Council's seven 
standing COITL.'11i ttees is G'le Corruni ttee on Leg islatiol1, composed 
of members of the Executive Council appointed by the President, 
The COnlIni ttee on Legislation pro;notes the policies and programs 
of the Federation in the State Legislature and makes reCO:T'u-uenda-
tions to the Executive Council (Constitution, Article XI Sectio'n 1). 
In 197-1, the follm'ling members of the Executive Counci 1 
registered as legislative advocates under former Government Code 
Section 9906: 
the President, the Secretary~Treasurer and one 
of the Vice Presidents. 
In 1975, under the provis~ons of the 
/y'Political Reform Act, the S2me three officers have registered 
/ 
as lobbyists for the Federation. 
.. 
_ a 
_ 
), 
.. 
Another major functlon of the Executive Council is to 
recommend endorsement of candidates and ballot me2sures to the 
Convention (Cons ',:,i tution, l.,.rticle XV, Part F) and to serve as 
the St:anding Corn.lui ttee on Poli tical Educa~:ion (hereinafter 
referred to as COPE) (Constitution, Article XIX, Section 1), 
The rules of the national AFL-CIO charge COPE with the 
following responsibilities: 
1. 
To encourage voter registration of members and 
their families. 
2. 
To insure maximum voting participation on election 
day. 
3. 
To encourage qualified candidates to seek public 
office,. 
4. 
To educate members and their families and develop 
comrnuni ty education programs on political issues 
through study, discussion and other appropriate 
means. 
5~ 
To support or oppose candidates 
(congressiona~ 
and state) and ballot propositions in both prlmary 
and general elections. 


, . 
No •. 75-004 
1 FPPC OPINIO)J'S 32 · 
l~age Five 
6. 
Assist in the effective solicitation Of individual 
voluntary contributions to the Political Contribu-
tions Commi ttee of the Com",i ttee on Poli t.ica 1 
Educa tion of the national J\l:'L-CIO. 
7. 
Carry out a statewide voter identificat.ion 
program in coo~eration with local central 
body COPES. 
III. 
We must first ccnsider whether the Federation itself, 
or any of its constituent parts such as the Executive Council, 
the COIrLmi ttee on Legislation or the Standing Com.mi ttee on 
Poli tica.l Education, is a "lobbyist l l! as t.hat tenn is defined 
in Section 82039. 
If the Federation were a lobbyist, then it 
would clearly be prohibited from rn~king contributions to state 
officials and to candidates for state office under Section 8E202, 
and to that extent its ability to engage in political activities 
would be impaired. 
. 
'l'he definition of fllobbyist" in Sect.ion 82039 includes 
...-..-'''any pc:rson" "'dho is cElployed to lobby 
If directly or through his 
/ 
agent.s." 
5i;1ce the Federation is unquestionably u "perso:1" as 
that term is defined in Section 82047, it has been suggested 
that the F·eclerat:ibn may be a -lobbyist because it is :'crn~:Jloyed" 
by it.s membership to lobby through its "agents," the individual 
lobbyist.s who represent the Federation. 
We conclude that neither the Federatio~, the Executive 
Council nor any of the Federation IS comIni ttees are lobbyists. 
~~e status of organizations like the Federation has been dealt 
vli t.ll specifically by a regulation of the CO:Tunission; 
\'lhen Cl lobbyist is employed or retained by 
a bona fide association, including any bonCl 
fide federation, confederation, or trade, 
labor, or membership organization, the asso-
ciation is the employer. of the lobbyist. 
2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18618 
(Emphasis added.) 
It is apparent, then, that although the Federation is 
an employer of lobbyists and is there 
required to file periodic 
reports under Section 86109, it is not itself a lobbyist and 
is therefore not prohibi t.ed t as an org ani za tio!1, from T:1aking 
con 
ioits. 
Similarly I the Executive Coun 
1 and t:he COITu-;1.i t-
Federation are not 10bhy5sts and are not subject, 
as org 
zations, to the prohibition of contributions. 


. . 
No. 75-004 
Page six 
IV. 
1 FPPC OPINIO~S 33 
Having established that the Federation, the Executive 
Council and the cor.~ittees are entitled, 2S organizations, to 
make contributions, we turn next to the questions raised by 
the fact that certain officers and staff members of the Federa-
tiOll are lobbyists and are thus personally subject to the pro-
hibitions. 
The Federation and COPL engage in a broad variety of 
political activities, some of which may b~ performed by its 
lobbyists. 
For example, to the extent the organization endorses, 
assists or makes contributions to federal candidates, Section 
86202 is inannlicable. 4/ Other nolitical activities may also 
_.L 
_ 
L 
_ 
'-
be outside the scooe of the prohibitions. 
We need not and do 
n~t now decide whe~, if ever: participation by a lobbyist in 
activities such as a voter registration or get-out-the-vote 
drive would be in violation of Section 86202. 
Such questions 
could turn on individual facts w~ich are not no~ before us. 
On the other hand, some political activities of the 
Federation are clearly proscribed for lobbyists. 
Certainly 
lobbyists may not participate in decisions to make contributions 
to state candidates and officials, act as an agent or interme-
. diary in the making of any such contribution I or to. arrange fo}: 
./,. the making of any such contribution .~/ Nor do \ve believe lobby-
/ 
ists may partici~ate in the procedures whereby the Federation 
endorses candidates for state office (or for non"state office if 
the candidate is' a state official). 
As "'le understand the pro-
cedures of t'liie Federation, an endorsement by the Convention is 
by rule or by practice a prerequisite to receiving a cash contri-
bution, and a significant percentage of the candidates who are 
endorsed receive cash contributions. 
All endorsed candidates 
are supported in literature published and distributed by and 
at the expense of the Federation. 
Under these circuJnstances, \'ie 
believe that the lobbyist's participation in the endorsement is 
closely enough related to the ultimate contribution that he may 
be said' to have 
II arranged" the contribution \·,i thin the meaning 
of Section 86202. 
Any contrary conclusion would undermine the 
purposes of Section 86202 by forcing candidates and office-
holders who hope to receive contributions from the Federation 
to curry the favor of the lobbyist in order to obtain the endorse-
ment which is a first essential step toward r2ceiving the con-
tribution. 
In this manner the personal influence of the lobbyist 
4/ 
. 
-
"Yle are advised that cash contributions to federal 
candidates are made by the national A.,1;'L-CIO and not by the 
Federation. 
On the other hand, the Federation's endorsements 
are no doubt considered by the national AFL-CIO, and the Federa-
tion distributes literature sU9Porting all endorsed candidates. 
PI 
-'l'he qu.estion of Hhat is "arranging" for a contribution 
is not addressed in this opinion except as it relates to endorse-
ment.?f cand~dates. 
For a complete discussion of "arranging 
for a contribution," see Opinion No. 75-098. 


Np. 75-004 
Page Seven 
OPINIONS 34 
which is traceable to control of money would be enhanced, in 
precisely the manner which the Political Reform Act is inten~ed 
to prohibit.,,?..! 
.. 
v. 
We have been asked specifically about staff members, 
officers and Executive Council members who are lobbyists. 
The staff of the Federation consists of nineteen full-
time paid persons and a General Counsel on a monthly retainer. 
At least two members of the staff are registered lobbyists as 
is the General Counsel. 
The staff are not voting merrbers of 
the Executive Council which recommends endorsement of candi-
dates to the convention and which also acts as COPE. 
Thus, 
the staff ,·Iho are lobbyists need rrot engage in the poli tical 
activities barred by this Act and as a matter of law may not 
engage in such activities. 
The Secretary-Treasurer, on the other hand, who is regis-
tered as a lobbyist, acts as the chief executive officer of the 
Federatiol1. 
According to the Constitution of the Federation, 
/.his duties include receiving all monies duc to the Fec1e:c a tion, 
/ 
keeping its books alld papers, maintaining the offices I 
cor:1piJi.ng 
an up-to-date list of the principal officers of each,affiliated 
organization, representing the Federation at all n 
ional con-' 
ventions, appointing representatives and directing their activi-
ties and employing staff. 
He is a member of the Execu~ive 
Council, and servos the role of Director of COPE, Secretary-
Treasurer of COPE and legislative represerltative of the Federa-
tion (Constitution, Article VII, Section I) . 
The President of the Federation, also registered as a 
lobbyist. presides at all conventions and meetings of the Execu-
tive Council, represents the Federation, appoints -
subject to 
the approval of the Executive COUI1Cil and the Convention -
the 
members of the various COI!lll1i ttees of the Federation I 
including 
the COfllmi tt:ee on Lcgislcd,:ion i 
and serves as Chairfllan of COPE: 
(Constitution, Article VI, Section I, and Rules Governing COPE 
(Rule 31). 
We do not conclude that it 1S always unlawful for 
lobbyists to participate in the making of endorsements by 
the organizations they represent. 
If the organization does 
not make contributions, or if it can be shown that there is 
no significant conncct:ion between the endorsc~nent and the 
likelihood of making a contribu 
on, the lobbyist's partici-
pation is not prohibited. 


Page Eight 
~ FPPC OPINIONS 35 
We conclude that the above-outlined roles of the Secre-
tary-Treasurer and the President are not compatible under the 
provisions of the Act. 
Although the making of contributions 
in state elections is not the sole activity of COPE, 
neit~er 
is it a peripheral activity. 
A'lobbyist is not permitted to 
Uarrange" such contributions. 
The Director and Chairman are 
responsible for the management and supervision of COPE~ and \~e 
do not believe it is realistic to expect them tu divorce them-
selves entirely from a major part of COPE's activit12S. 
Pre-
sumably, they are responsible for the hiring and firing of COPE 
staff. 
Even if they delegate decisions regarding contributions 
to members of the staff, it is almost inevitable that the staff 
will respond to the actual or perceived desires of the Director 
and Chairman. 
Furthermore, the state official who receives a 
contribution from COPE is likely to assume, rightly or wrongly, 
that the contribution \-las ordered by the Director or the Chairman, 
thus enhancing their influence as lobbyists in precisely the manner 
that the Act seeks to prevent. 
For these reasons we find that 
the Secretary-Treasurer and the P£esident of the Federation cannot 
continue to act both as lobbyists and hold these positions in 
COPE. 7/ 
The Commission cannot and s~ould not outlir:e the appropri-
ate organizational structure for the Federation. 
The Federation 
,.~must decide \-lhether it wishes its key officers~o continue as 
/' 
lobbyists or '.-1hether these persons should cont±liue' to participate 
in its political endorsements and decisiorisori'political contri-
butions. ' Under t.he terms 'of: the Political Reform Ac::,t, tl1e Fe.dera-
tion's officers may not perform both functions. 
The General Vice President represents the Federation and 
works under the direction and suoervision of the Secretarv-
Treasurer (Constitution, Article-VIII, Section 1). 
Since'he 
is not a registered lobbyist, none of 
his activ.~ties are re-
stricted. 
This person IS, activi ties. relating _t:o' contributions 
in state political races may not be supervised or directed by 
the Secretary-Treasurer. 
At' the present time, only O!le member of the Executive 
Council, other than the President and Secretary-Treasurer, is 
a registered lobbyist, and he is also a paid member of the 
staff of the Federation. 
We are informed by the Federation 
that it is not anticipated that other members of the Executive 
Council will qualify as lobbyists. 
2/ 
We note that the national rules governing COPE do not 
require the Secretary-Treasurer to be Director of COPE. 
See 
AFL-CIO, Rules Governing Co~~ittees on Political Education of 
State and Local Central Bodies, Rule 30. 
,. 


~ . 
N,o. 75-004 
. Page Nine 
1 FPPC OPINIONS 36 
The members of the Executive Council who are not lobby-
ists may continue to perform all activities without restriction. 
The one member who is a lobbyist may not engage in activities 
prohibited by Section 86202 of the Act and may not continue to 
serve on COPE.

members of the Executive Councilor officers of the Federation

sections.conclusion: Nothing in the Political Reform Act prevents the Federation from continuing to participate fully in the political process. This is so whether or not members of the staff, members of the Executive Council or officers of the Federation act as lobbyists. However, those individuals who are lobbyists may not participate in any activities of the Federation which come within the prohibitions of Government Code Section 86202 and may not serve on COPE, the Federation's political arm.

III. 
We must first ccnsider whether the Federation itself, 
or any of its constituent parts such as the Executive Council, 
the COIrLmi ttee on Legislation or the Standing Com.mi ttee on 
Poli tica.l Education, is a "lobbyist l l! as t.hat tenn is defined 
in Section 82039. 
If the Federation were a lobbyist, then it 
would clearly be prohibited from rn~king contributions to state 
officials and to candidates for state office under Section 8E202, 
and to that extent its ability to engage in political activities 
would be impaired. 
.

If directly or through his 
/ 
agent.s."

that the F·eclerat:ibn may be a -lobbyist because it is :'crn~:Jloyed" 
by it.s membership to lobby through its "agents," the individual

We conclude that neither the Federatio~, the Executive 
Council nor any of the Federation IS comIni ttees are lobbyists. 
~~e status of organizations like the Federation has been dealt

fide federation, confederation, or trade,

ciation is the employer. of the lobbyist.

zations, to the prohibition of contributions.

Having established that the Federation, the Executive 
Council and the cor.~ittees are entitled, 2S organizations, to 
make contributions, we turn next to the questions raised by 
the fact that certain officers and staff members of the Federa-
tiOll are lobbyists and are thus personally subject to the pro-
hibitions. 
The Federation and COPL engage in a broad variety of 
political activities, some of which may b~ performed by its 
lobbyists. 
For example, to the extent the organization endorses, 
assists or makes contributions to federal candidates, Section 
86202 is inannlicable. 4/ Other nolitical activities may also

be outside the scooe of the prohibitions. 
We need not and do 
n~t now decide whe~, if ever: participation by a lobbyist in

drive would be in violation of Section 86202. 
Such questions 
could turn on individual facts w~ich are not no~ before us. 
On the other hand, some political activities of the 
Federation are clearly proscribed for lobbyists. 
Certainly 
lobbyists may not participate in decisions to make contributions 
to state candidates and officials, act as an agent or interme-
. diary in the making of any such contribution I or to. arrange fo}: 
./,. the making of any such contribution .~/ Nor do \ve believe lobby-
/ 
ists may partici~ate in the procedures whereby the Federation 
endorses candidates for state office (or for non"state office if 
the candidate is' a state official). 
As "'le understand the pro-
cedures of t'liie Federation, an endorsement by the Convention is 
by rule or by practice a prerequisite to receiving a cash contri-
bution, and a significant percentage of the candidates who are 
endorsed receive cash contributions. 
All endorsed candidates 
are supported in literature published and distributed by and 
at the expense of the Federation. 
Under these circuJnstances, \'ie 
believe that the lobbyist's participation in the endorsement is 
closely enough related to the ultimate contribution that he may 
be said' to have 
II arranged" the contribution \·,i thin the meaning 
of Section 86202. 
Any contrary conclusion would undermine the 
purposes of Section 86202 by forcing candidates and office-
holders who hope to receive contributions from the Federation 
to curry the favor of the lobbyist in order to obtain the endorse-
ment which is a first essential step toward r2ceiving the con-
tribution. 
In this manner the personal influence of the lobbyist

Federation. 
On the other hand, the Federation's endorsements 
are no doubt considered by the national AFL-CIO, and the Federa-
tion distributes literature sU9Porting all endorsed candidates. 
PI

for a contribution," see Opinion No. 75-098.

which is traceable to control of money would be enhanced, in 
precisely the manner which the Political Reform Act is inten~ed 
to prohibit.,,?..!

v. 
We have been asked specifically about staff members, 
officers and Executive Council members who are lobbyists. 
The staff of the Federation consists of nineteen full-
time paid persons and a General Counsel on a monthly retainer. 
At least two members of the staff are registered lobbyists as 
is the General Counsel. 
The staff are not voting merrbers of 
the Executive Council which recommends endorsement of candi-
dates to the convention and which also acts as COPE. 
Thus, 
the staff ,·Iho are lobbyists need rrot engage in the poli tical 
activities barred by this Act and as a matter of law may not 
engage in such activities. 
The Secretary-Treasurer, on the other hand, who is regis-
tered as a lobbyist, acts as the chief executive officer of the 
Federatiol1.

ventions, appointing representatives and directing their activi-
ties and employing staff. 
He is a member of the Execu~ive 
Council, and servos the role of Director of COPE, Secretary-
Treasurer of COPE and legislative represerltative of the Federa-
tion (Constitution, Article VII, Section I) . 
The President of the Federation, also registered as a 
lobbyist. presides at all conventions and meetings of the Execu-
tive Council, represents the Federation, appoints -
subject to 
the approval of the Executive COUI1Cil and the Convention -
the 
members of the various COI!lll1i ttees of the Federation I 
including 
the COfllmi tt:ee on Lcgislcd,:ion i 
and serves as Chairfllan of COPE: 
(Constitution, Article VI, Section I, and Rules Governing COPE 
(Rule 31). 
We do not conclude that it 1S always unlawful for 
lobbyists to participate in the making of endorsements by 
the organizations they represent. 
If the organization does 
not make contributions, or if it can be shown that there is 
no significant conncct:ion between the endorsc~nent and the 
likelihood of making a contribu 
on, the lobbyist's partici-
pation is not prohibited.

We conclude that the above-outlined roles of the Secre-
tary-Treasurer and the President are not compatible under the 
provisions of the Act. 
Although the making of contributions 
in state elections is not the sole activity of COPE, 
neit~er 
is it a peripheral activity. 
A'lobbyist is not permitted to 
Uarrange" such contributions. 
The Director and Chairman are 
responsible for the management and supervision of COPE~ and \~e 
do not believe it is realistic to expect them tu divorce them-
selves entirely from a major part of COPE's activit12S. 
Pre-
sumably, they are responsible for the hiring and firing of COPE 
staff. 
Even if they delegate decisions regarding contributions 
to members of the staff, it is almost inevitable that the staff 
will respond to the actual or perceived desires of the Director 
and Chairman. 
Furthermore, the state official who receives a 
contribution from COPE is likely to assume, rightly or wrongly, 
that the contribution \-las ordered by the Director or the Chairman, 
thus enhancing their influence as lobbyists in precisely the manner 
that the Act seeks to prevent. 
For these reasons we find that 
the Secretary-Treasurer and the P£esident of the Federation cannot 
continue to act both as lobbyists and hold these positions in 
COPE. 7/ 
The Commission cannot and s~ould not outlir:e the appropri-
ate organizational structure for the Federation. 
The Federation

lobbyists or '.-1hether these persons should cont±liue' to participate 
in its political endorsements and decisiorisori'political contri-
butions. ' Under t.he terms 'of: the Political Reform Ac::,t, tl1e Fe.dera-
tion's officers may not perform both functions. 
The General Vice President represents the Federation and 
works under the direction and suoervision of the Secretarv-
Treasurer (Constitution, Article-VIII, Section 1). 
Since'he 
is not a registered lobbyist, none of 
his activ.~ties are re-
stricted. 
This person IS, activi ties. relating _t:o' contributions 
in state political races may not be supervised or directed by 
the Secretary-Treasurer. 
At' the present time, only O!le member of the Executive 
Council, other than the President and Secretary-Treasurer, is 
a registered lobbyist, and he is also a paid member of the 
staff of the Federation. 
We are informed by the Federation 
that it is not anticipated that other members of the Executive 
Council will qualify as lobbyists. 
2/ 
We note that the national rules governing COPE do not 
require the Secretary-Treasurer to be Director of COPE.

The members of the Executive Council who are not lobby-
ists may continue to perform all activities without restriction.

sections.question_synthetic: The California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO asks whether its members, officers, and staff who are registered lobbyists can continue to participate in the Federation's political activities, including endorsements and campaign contributions, through its standing committee on political education (COPE).
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Federation as an organization can continue its political activities and make contributions, but individual lobbyists employed by the Federation are prohibited from participating in contribution-making activities or serving on COPE, as they are subject to the ban on lobbyists making political contributions under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document is Opinion No. 75-004 dated June 18, 1975. Clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections present. Document is slightly OCR-corrupted in places (e.g., 'H.ules' for 'Rules', 'P~' fragment) but sections are intact and readable. Full ANALYSIS section provided. High confidence in extraction.

QUESTION: s by Charles Scully, General Counsel of the California 
Labor Federation, AFL-CIO (lithe FfOderation") : 
1. 
If both officers and member s of t.he staff of 
the Federation are lobbyists, may the Federa-
t.ion, through its standing corn .. raittee on political 
education, engage in its traditional political 
act.ivi ties? 
2. 
If tJ1G three salaried officers of the Fecieration, 
namely, the President, the Secretary-Treasurer 
and the General Vice President, are lobb~istsf 
may n\e Federa tlOh, through its stanciing cOI:J:d. t.tee· 
on political education, engage in its traditional 
political acti vi t.ies? 
3 •. If members of the Executive Council of the Federa-
tion are lobbyists, may the Federation, through 
:its st.anding COltu'l1i ttee on political educa tioH, 
engage in its traditional political activities? 
4~ 
If the answer to each of the first three questions 
is negative, then is there any manner in which 
the Federation may continue to engage in its 
traditional political activities and also continue 
to attempt to influence legislative and cdl:nnlstra-
tive cction? 
Rclevant information concerning the structure of t~2 
Federation is contained in the analysis. 
cm:JCLUSIO?:J 
Nothing in the Poli tical Reform l\ct pn:::vents the Federa-
tion from continuing to participate fully in the political 
process. 
This is so t,oJht...:t:her or not mernbcrs of the staff I 
" 


. . 
NO. 
1~-UU.q 
Page Two 
. . 
members of the Executive Councilor officers of the Federation 
act as lobbyists. 
However, those individuals who are lobbyists 
may not participate in any activities of the Federation which" 
come \-lithin the prohibitions of .Governrnent Code Section 86202 1 / 
and may not serve on COPE, the Federation's political arm.

CONCLUSION: Nothing in the Political Reform Act prevents the Federation from continuing to participate fully in the political process. This is so whether or not members of the staff, members of the Executive Council or officers of the Federation act as lobbyists. However, those individuals who are lobbyists may not participate in any activities of the Federation which come within the prohibitions of Government Code Section 86202 and may not serve on COPE, the Federation's political arm.

In the Matter of: ) ) O~inion requested by ) California Labor Federation, ) AFL-CIO ) No. 75-004 June 18, 1975 BY THE cm·~lISSION: He have been asked the following questions by Charles Scully, General Counsel of the California Labor Federation, AFL-CIO (lithe FfOderation") : 1. If both officers and member s of t.he staff of the Federation are lobbyists, may the Federa- t.ion, through its standing corn .. raittee on political education, engage in its traditional political act.ivi ties? 2. If tJ1G three salaried officers of the Fecieration, namely, the President, the Secretary-Treasurer and the General Vice President, are lobb~istsf may n\e Federa tlOh, through its stanciing cOI:J:d. t.tee· on political education, engage in its traditional political acti vi t.ies? 3 •. If members of the Executive Council of the Federa- tion are lobbyists, may the Federation, through :its st.anding COltu'l1i ttee on political educa tioH, engage in its traditional political activities? 4~ If the answer to each of the first three questions is negative, then is there any manner in which the Federation may continue to engage in its traditional political activities and also continue to attempt to influence legislative and cdl:nnlstra- tive cction? Rclevant information concerning the structure of t~2 Federation is contained in the analysis. cm:JCLUSIO?:J Nothing in the Poli tical Reform l\ct pn:::vents the Federa- tion from continuing to participate fully in the political process. This is so t,oJht...:t:her or not mernbcrs of the staff I " . . NO. 1~-UU.q Page Two . . members of the Executive Councilor officers of the Federation act as lobbyists. However, those individuals who are lobbyists may not participate in any activities of the Federation which" come \-lithin the prohibitions of .Governrnent Code Section 86202 1 / and may not serve on COPE, the Federation's political arm. ANALYSIS I. The questions we have been asked are important not only because of the large size of the Federation and the important role it has historically performed in political carnpaigns , ij but also because the questions call for elaboration of the fun- damental nature of the regulation' of lobbyists embodied in the Political Reform Act. In essence, we are asked whether it is possible uI,der the Act for a mass membership organization to represent the interests of its meIT~ers before the Legislature and administrative agencies and at the sa.rne time make contribu- tions to state officials and candidates and otherv:ise rer:1ain ~activ~ in the political process~ As we shall_see; the regula- /' tion of lobbyists ir:1posed by the _L\ct I ,-.-hich protects important interests of the public in assuring integrity and impartiality in government, is fully consistent with the continu~tion by the Federation of its traditional lobbying and political activities . The Political Reform Act attempts to diminish the improper personal influence of lobbyists by prohibiting them from making contributions to state officials and candidates. . 1/ It shall be unla'.-;ful for a lobbyist to make a contribution
embedding.summary: FPPC Opinion 75-004 addresses whether the California Labor Federation can engage in political activities when its officers and staff are registered lobbyists. The FPPC concludes that the organization itself can continue full political participation and make contributions, but individual lobbyists cannot participate in contribution activities or serve on the Federation's political committee (COPE).
